title: Death rate by cause
subtitle: The estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people from broad categories of causes of death. Injuries (such as accidents, violence and suicides); communicable, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases; and [non-communicable diseases](#dod:non-communicable-diseases).
caption:
Death rate by cause

The estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people from broad categories of causes of death. Injuries (such as accidents, violence and suicides); communicable, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases; and [non-communicable diseases](#dod:non-communicable-diseases).

This stacked area chart tracks the global estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people across three broad cause categories from 1980 to 2023, using age-standardized data to ensure valid comparisons over time. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 1,400 deaths per 100,000, while the horizontal axis spans four decades. The bottom layer, colored pink, represents non-communicable diseases (NCDs); the middle green layer covers communicable, maternal, neonatal, and nutritional diseases; and the top tan layer denotes injuries. In 1980, the total death rate stood near 1,300 per 100,000, with NCDs accounting for roughly 800, communicable and related diseases adding about 400, and injuries contributing approximately 100. By 2023, the total had fallen to around 750 per 100,000. The decline is driven primarily by reductions in communicable diseases, which dropped from about 400 to under 200 over the period. Non-communicable diseases also decreased, falling from roughly 800 to just above 500. Injuries remained relatively flat, hovering between 80 and 120 throughout, with a visible spike around 2020 pushing the total briefly above 800 before receding. The green band narrows steadily across the timeline, while the pink band contracts more gradually. The tan band stays thin and stable except for the temporary rise near the end. The most visible pattern is the sustained reduction in total mortality, led by falling rates of communicable and non-communicable diseases, with injuries showing minimal long-term change.
